Is artificial intelligence good for society? The short answer is Yes. Artificial intelligence or AI will bring many changes to the world that will make things better, including medical innovations, improving public health, increasing productivity for ordinary people, and making it easier for governments and officials to detect and respond to longterm trends. Artificial intelligence can also help us to fight climate change which is a global problem. Overall, I see AI as something that should not be marginalized in its advancements if we want to move things forward as a society. Ultimately, it will be embraced across the world in all different countries so rather than considering whether or not it is good for society, we should focus on how to implement AI ethically. With the proper rules and safeguards on AI as agreed upon by world governments, we can make sure that AI is indeed good for society.

Animals are living beings that can feel pain.  They are unable to consent to their bodies being used for scientific research. Government surveillance is absolutely necessary for national security. Prior to 2001, the U.S. had a significant national security structure to prevent terrorism and promote national security, but it as the events of September 11th, 2001 showed, it was not enough. Things like the Patriot Act enabled the U.S. government to create a massive digital filter, suctioning up great amounts of data to sift through and prevent any potential terrorist acts from occurring. The greater sharing promoted by the September 11th attacks also served to ensure the intelligence made it to the right places to stop any events before they occurred. Additionally, the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act served to promote further digital surveillance of personnel suspected of terrorism related crimes, further empowering the government to proactively prevent these acts before they occur. Government surveillance is critical to the success of our continued national security.

School uniforms should be required as they level the playing field and make it so that students can focus on studies. Children can still express themselves creatively during their off hours, but with school uniforms, students can focus on studying.  This also allows a student to separate school from leisure, which will allow them to enter "work mode" while learning and more effectively relax in their off hours, leading to a better school-life balance.  Requiring school uniforms also eliminates the burden of dress codes and various violations including rude or crude images or text, torn or dirty clothing, or clothing of inappropriate length or coverage. Government surveillance used for National Security purposes has been around for years.  I believe it has bipartisan support in Congress.  It is monitored partially through the use of the FISA courts.  It has resulted in arguably a safer US.  Discontinuing is use would be a gift to our adversaries.  When it is supported in the 3 branches of government t needs to remain.

Government surveillance is not necessary for national security, and in fact proves counterproductive.  The greatest threat against America comes from the actions of lone actors, who can evade detection from necessarily broad-focused surveillance technologies.  Moreover, the act of surveilling its own citizens more intensely creates an atmosphere of distrust, itself exacerbating tensions in this country.  I'm also not convinced that America would be skillful at surveilling its citizens to detect threats.  We have not been able to detect complex plots in the past.  Moreover, some agencies, like the CIA, are not allowed to spy on domestic persons, by law.  Lastly, the utility of surveillance would come from its analysis.  Who would sift through mountains of data (much of which the government already has access to)?  How would they make threat determinations?  How could they act preemptively against perceived threats?  I don't see our security apparatus as able to carry out thees missions iwth available resources.

The entire point of our school system is to teach things to our students. As a result, some form of testing is required. We also need to test every student the same way, so that the student themselves is the only variable. For example, if student a has learned 90% of the information and student B has learned 60% of the information it is logical that student a will score higher on a standardized test. We can also use the average score of all the students to answer the question is "the current curriculum an effective way to teach this information?" If almost every student scores poorly on the standardized test, we can conclude that the curriculum is not effective.

So the standardized tests are not only for the students, but also in the classroom and the curriculum itself. If we have an ineffective teaching method or an ineffective lesson plan, we need a way to be aware of that, so corrections can be made.

As an advocate for considering race in college admissions to ensure diversity, I firmly believe in the value of a diverse educational environment. From my perspective, when colleges include race as one of the factors in their admissions process, it's not just about numbers or meeting quotas; it’s about enriching the educational experience for everyone.

I've seen firsthand how diverse classrooms lead to more dynamic and comprehensive discussions. In an increasingly globalized world, being exposed to a variety of perspectives and backgrounds is essential. It prepares us not just academically, but also socially, for the real world where we will interact with people from all walks of life.

Moreover, it's important to acknowledge the historical context. For decades, certain racial and ethnic groups have been underrepresented in higher education due to systemic barriers and inequalities. By considering race in admissions, colleges can play a role in leveling the playing field and offering opportunities to those who have been historically marginalized.

This isn't just about benefiting minority students; it's about benefiting all students. When we interact with people who are different from us, we learn tolerance, empathy, and gain a broader understanding of the world. It prepares us to be better leaders, innovators, and citizens.

Finally, the legal framework in many places, including the U.S. Supreme Court, has recognized the importance of diversity in education and upheld the limited use of race as a factor in admissions. This is a testament to the value our society places on diversity as a crucial component of education.

It may perpetuate discrimination by treating individuals differently based on their race. Admissions should focus on merit and individual achievements rather than factors beyond an applicant's control. A truly diverse and equitable system should prioritize socioeconomic factors, ensuring opportunities for underprivileged individuals, rather than solely relying on race as a diversity indicator

Standardized testing disrupts that natural course load of the teacher, taking away from the natural flow of learning. The US should ban fossil fuels to combat climate change. The US is one of the largest enablers of climate change, and as a result, has a moral responsibility to help slow it down. Banning fossil fuels is one of the most concrete things the US can do, with an immediate impact.  If we let it continue going untouched, the temperature will continue to rise as it has been doing so, causing even more severe weather patterns to the US itself like the tornados in Tennessee, or the dangerously hot temperatures that happened in Phoenix over the summer; as a result, each time that the US delays taking action, such as banning fossil fuels, it is causing instability to its own country, and the loss of American lives.

The US should diminish use of fossil fuels, and in the future that can perhaps evolve into an outright BAN, yes, but the question asks whether the fuels should be banned, implying NOW, and I don't think that would be wise. Too much of our economy depends on fossil fuel -- sales of the fuel, automobiles, deliver of products. 

We should, rather than ban, discourage the use of fossil fuels, by gradually making them very expensive, hastening the use of non-fossil fuels, while encouraging use of sustainable forms of fuel. There is a lot of work to do still on batteries -- they are resource intensive, and require environment-damaging mining. So we can improve alternative fuels while gradually discouraging the use of fossil fuels. 

So: I say discourage rather than ban. Banning would be too disruptive.

Online learning provides new opportunities to those who may not have access to any education otherwise. It also opens up the opportunity to learn at one's own pace. People with jobs or with kids will have more access to continued education and can use that to improve their socioeconomic status or just their knowledge. With the improvement of technology and the larger accessibility, it is clear that online learning could replace traditional in-person education.

Online learning is not a suitable replacement for in person education. Though online learning can be extremely effective and efficient, there is no substitute for being face to face with an instructor and getting personal attention from them as well as being able to act and react and ask questions in a real space. People who do online learning instead of in-person may miss out on the focus that being there in the same space with someone can provide. It is easier to understand and pay attention when you are listening to and engaged with someone in real life. It is difficult to simulate that type of interaction online because we are limited visually to a small screen.

There are many reasons that online learning is not a suitable replacement for traditional in-person education. The first and probably most important reason being socializing our children. In-person schooling is for much more than just learning basic skills like math and science, it is for learning how to interact with others. We learn to interact with our peers and make friends, we learn how to interact with authority figures in our teachers, and we learn to interact with students in different age groups than your own. As long as life requires us to have interactions in real life, and not just over the internet, in-person schooling will always be important because of the social factors. Another reason in-person school is better than online is because of the rise of cheating with tools like AI. Cheating is much more difficult to do in the classroom with a teacher right there with you than at your own home on your own computer. Obviously it can still happen, but it is certainly less likely to happen.

Global warming isn't called "regional", "local", or "national" warming - it's global because it is an international issue that affects everyone on the planet. We should work together to solve problems that impact us together. This isn't to say that countries who might not be able to abide by certain guidelines should face struggles, but rather, that other countries should ensure they can and provide aid where necessary. As a whole, the world is a community, and the ideas of nations and borders is outdated, impractical, and just overall quite silly.

Any policies agreed upon on an international level will be surface level and thus be highly ineffective. The need to address climate change is real and necessary and we must take effective action quickly. That can NOT be done on an international level. That MUST be done on a domestic level.

AI's rapid rise brings both immense potential and critical risks:

Job losses: Automation through AI could displace millions, particularly in repetitive jobs. Think: of self-driving trucks displacing drivers.

Biased algorithms: AI trained on biased data can perpetuate discrimination in areas like law enforcement and hiring.

Black box AI: Many AI systems lack transparency, making it difficult to understand how they make decisions and raising concerns about fairness and accountability.

Privacy threats: The widespread use of AI for surveillance and data collection raises concerns about individual freedoms and potential misuse.

Existential risk: While speculative, some experts warn about the possibility of AI surpassing human control and posing a threat to humanity.

These risks are not inherent to AI, but potential pitfalls we must address through responsible development and regulation. By openly discussing and mitigating these concerns, we can ensure AI benefits all, without jeopardizing our values or well-being.

The question of whether college should be tuition-free is a complex and debated issue with various perspectives. Advocates argue that a tuition-free college system could enhance accessibility, alleviate economic burdens, and promote social equality. They contend that such a model could lead to a more educated and skilled workforce, contributing to economic growth and innovation.Opponents, on the other hand, express concerns about the potential increase in taxes and the impact on the quality of education. The debate extends to the role of federal investments, state contributions, and the overall cost of public colleges and universities.

This would cause countless new students to not even reach entry-level pre requisite courses. This would cause students to never reach college, as life may progress and prevent them from being able to attend.

I think online learning can be a suitable replacement for traditional in-person education, but only for certain groups of learners who are amenable to the online format, as well as only for certain fields of study. This proposition also depends on what we mean by "suitable replacement" -- as in, is it just merely sufficient, or is it actually better/an improvement? I think those are two different claims that require two different defenses. I'm just here to defend the claim that it's merely sufficient or on par with traditional in-person education for some people in some subjects.

The Electoral College should no longer be used. Further, the disuniformity in how states allocate their electoral votes only creates a messier process, with some states giving winner take all and others dividing votes by district. The purpose of the electors themselves is also moot, as the issue of "faithless" electors only causes more issues, especially when they can't even be used for their original purpose. Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico have large populations with millions of residents who lack full voting representation in the U.S. Congress. Granting them statehood would ensure equal representation for their citizens, aligning with the democratic principles of equal rights and representation.
Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico have large populations with millions of residents who lack full voting representation in the U.S. Congress. Granting them statehood would ensure equal representation for their citizens, aligning with the democratic principles of equal rights and representation.
Statehood for Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico would empower their residents to govern themselves more independently, make local decisions that directly impact their communities, and have greater control over their economic, social, and political destinies.
Both Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico have a long history of advocating for statehood and seeking equal rights and recognition within the United States. Granting them statehood would be a step towards rectifying historical disparities and honoring their aspirations for full inclusion in the American political system.
In conclusion, granting statehood to Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico aligns with principles of democracy, representation, self-determination, and equality, providing their residents with the full rights and responsibilities inherent in U.S. statehood.

Space exploration is undeniably a worthwhile investment for humanity. Firstly, it drives technological innovation. Many of the technologies we use in our daily lives, such as GPS, satellite communication, and medical imaging, have their roots in space exploration research and development. By investing in space exploration, we push the boundaries of what's possible technologically, leading to advancements that benefit society as a whole.

Furthermore, space exploration fosters international collaboration and cooperation. Projects like the International Space Station (ISS) bring together scientists, engineers, and astronauts from various countries, promoting diplomacy and understanding across borders. This collaboration not only enhances our ability to explore space but also strengthens global partnerships for addressing common challenges on Earth.

Space exploration also expands our understanding of the universe and our place within it. Through missions to other planets, moons, and beyond, we gain insights into planetary science, astrobiology, and the fundamental laws of physics. These discoveries not only satisfy our innate curiosity but also have practical implications, such as understanding the potential for life beyond Earth and assessing threats from asteroids or comets.

Moreover, investing in space exploration inspires the next generation of scientists, engineers, and explorers. The pursuit of space exploration captures the imagination of people around the world, encouraging students to pursue careers in STEM fields and pushing humanity forward through innovation and discovery.

In summary, space exploration offers numerous benefits to humanity, from driving technological innovation and fostering international cooperation to expanding our understanding of the universe and inspiring future generations. As we continue to invest in space exploration, we not only push the boundaries of human knowledge and capabilities but also ensure a brighter future for all.

Hi Everyone, I strongly oppose the idea that felons should be granted the right to vote. 

Voting is a right reserved for law-abiding citizens who make meaningful contributions to the community. Felons, by definition, have broken our society's laws and are therefore ineligible to enjoy some rights, including the ability to vote. Allowing them to engage in the political process sends the incorrect message and undermines the importance of citizenship. 

Furthermore, restoring voting rights to criminals without adequate protections jeopardizes the integrity of our electoral system. Felons may still have strong interests that conflict with the public good, and their involvement in elections may distort the results in favor of their own agenda. 

While I recognize the significance of rehabilitation and reintegration into society, providing criminals voting rights contradicts the concepts of justice and accountability.

I believe that extended amounts of internet access could be affecting people's intelligence, or at the very least at surface levels. Lately, all anyone ever seems to care about is what the most popular celebrity is doing or what the new tiktok trend is. There is nothing wrong with using social media to keep in touch with family and friends, but there are some who take it too far and prove what can happen to society if we spend too much time on social media and stop interacting face to face. I have spoken to friends and coworkers who's life revolves around social media and it is concerning. She has to have the latest trends, whether it is makeup or a dress or a corset. She goes out to "hang out", but only takes pictures to post them on social media and then doesn't get off of her phone the entire rest of the night. It it making people be less social.

Social media has opened a significant line of communication amongst people. It allows people to connect with others on a whole new level from many different places. This also means that it provides the opportunity to make educational videos and posts that reaches thousands and thousands of people. I personally have seen this opportunity taken by professionals who create positive and educational content to share with the masses and ensures that not only is social media used for entertainment but learning as well.

Animals should not be used for scientific research because they are living beings who cannot consent to undergoing this testing. We do not use humans for research because it is dangerous, could cause injury/death, and moral reasons. These moral reasons also apply to animals, as they are living creatures with consciousness, identity, and feelings. In addition, information learned from animal testing does not always translate into humans, which creates a risk of faulty clinical trials because of the possibility for imprecise results. Lastly, most research and testing done on animals is performed merely out of curiosity and does not have any actual meaningful implications or scientific contributions.

Standardized testing should be required in schools because there has to be a way to determine everyone's skill level. By having standardized testing, where every student studies the same material and is given the same questions and amount of time to take the test, we can use the results of the tests to better determine what needs to be taught more/less and who needs more help in certain areas. It is simply a good way to have a base standard to go from when determining the academic strengths and weaknesses of students.

Elected or appointed government officials should not be paid the minimum wage. A big reason for this is that the current minimum wage — whether it be state or federal — is not high enough to correspond with work of that caliber. In other words, given that these people are to represent regular citizens and engage in government activities, not many people will be enticed to apply with such a low-paying position.

Artificial intelligence is not good for society.  One of the main reasons is the job loss that it was cause in a variety of industries.  Another reason is that the AI is only as "smart" as the person doing the coding.  AI only knows what people tell it to know and what it gathers online.  In today's political climate and with our nation being so divided, I fear that AI will be biased towards one side, simply because one side controls the narrative.  Further, AI tools do not have morals or ethics and are incapable of navigating complex issues that may arise that need a human touch.
